1	# Friction log
2	
3	Status: Current
4	Convention: append-only within a session
5	
6	Raw material for the session-start tooling retro (see the STANDING ITEM in `CLAUDE.md`).
7	Friction is logged **as it happens**, not reconstructed later - a retro that runs on memory
8	is a memory test, and this shop's whole thesis is that memory evaporates nightly.
9	
10	Log anything that cost time, produced a wrong diagnosis, or made a defect possible: a tool
11	that lied, a default that bit, a missing capability, a command that had to be retried, a
12	manual step that should be mechanical. Small entries are fine; the point is the pattern
13	across many, not the severity of any one.
14	
15	Format: `date | what happened | what it cost | class`.
16	
17	---
18	
19	## 2026-07-21 / 2026-07-22 - founding session
20	
21	| When | Friction | Cost | Class |
22	|---|---|---|---|
23	| 07-21 | `board.ps1 add 3 Backlog` rejected: project 6's Status field has only `Todo / In Progress / Done`. The board cannot represent `In Review`, which the ladder requires, or a holding yard. | One failed command; a process state the board cannot express | Missing capability in a data source |
24	| 07-21 | Branch protection applied with `enforce_admins: false`; API read-back looked perfect; the guard was decorative because agents authenticate as the owner. | ~90s + a junk commit on public `main` + a revert PR | Guard unverified until fault-injected |
25	| 07-22 | PowerShell 5.1 escaping: a backtick-n inside a double-quoted string was a parser error. | 1 round trip | Shell defaults |
26	| 07-22 | `git revert -q` - `-q` is not a valid revert flag; the revert silently did not happen and the branch was pushed unchanged. | 1 round trip + a wrong push | Tool flag assumed |
27	| 07-22 | Variables `$a` / `$b` in a PowerShell one-liner collided such that `Remove-Item $a,$b` resolved to drive `A:` and was blocked. | 1 round trip, whole command lost | Shell defaults |
28	| 07-22 | `Get-Content` defaults to ANSI in PS 5.1 and silently mangled every non-ASCII character in an extracted verdict. Made the word VERBATIM false while everything appeared to work. | 1 defect, caught only by a byte-count cross-check | Encoding defaults |
29	| 07-22 | `Set-Content -Encoding utf8` writes a BOM and converts LF to CRLF, so the script hashed one string and wrote another. Every landed file failed its own verifier. | 1 defect, 1 debugging cycle | Encoding defaults |
30	| 07-22 | Every single commit warns `LF will be replaced by CRLF`. Real warnings are buried in the noise. | Continuous noise on every commit | Missing repo config |
31	| 07-22 | Task output files (`tasks/<id>.output`) are **0 bytes**. I assumed the transcript was there, diagnosed data loss, and started hand-transcribing a 46k-char verdict. | A wrong diagnosis and a near-miss on a hand-maintained mirror | Assumed a capability without checking |
32	| 07-22 | `entire` CLI exits non-zero on successful `checkpoint explain`, so exit codes cannot gate on it. | Minor; needed output-based success detection | Tool contract |
33	| 07-22 | First provenance citation used the Entire session UUID - not a resolvable key. Dead on arrival. | Caught only by following the citation before landing | Untested reference |
34	| 07-22 | ~~GitNexus stale-index warning fires on every Bash call... Cross-project tooling leak~~ **WITHDRAWN by the owner, same day.** `docs/setup.md:35` already anticipates this: the GitNexus index is trigger-gated on FIRST CODE landing, because there is nothing to index before that. The nag is expected and self-resolving, not a leak. **The retro misclassified a documented, correctly-deferred state as friction** - the log's first false positive, kept visible rather than deleted, because an instrument that cries wolf is worse than no instrument and this one did on its first run. Lesson for the retro itself: check `docs/setup.md`'s trigger-gated table BEFORE classifying a tool's noise as friction. | Zero - it was working as designed | **RETRO FALSE POSITIVE** |
35	| 07-22 | No CI exists, so `CLAUDE.md`'s definition of "verified" (the pipeline that ships it went green) is unachievable by any car. Three guards are parked on it (#3, #4, #6). | Structural: verification honesty is currently aspirational | Missing capability |
36	| 07-22 | Landing a verdict takes 7 hand-typed parameters and a remembered command. | Per-artifact manual cost; the class the harness train exists to close | Manual step that should be mechanical |
37	| 07-22 | I leaned on multiple-choice menus during open design discussion; the owner twice redirected wanting to talk it through. | 2 wasted round trips | Interaction pattern, not tooling |

39	## Retro #1 outcome (2026-07-22)
40	
41	Installed, all free and off-the-shelf, all verified rather than assumed:
42	
43	| Install | Closes | Proof |
44	|---|---|---|
45	| `.gitattributes` | The CRLF warning on every commit; protects hashed verdicts from filter rewrites on a fresh clone | All 3 verdicts still verify after the change |
46	| PowerShell 7.6.3 | The encoding class - 3 defects, all inside the integrity tooling | Tested directly: no BOM on write, non-ASCII round-trips intact, `&&` works |
47	| GitHub Actions CI | "Verified" was unachievable by our own definition; Pester tests nobody ran; `Verify-Verdict` running only on memory | Green run 29905304676 (19 tests, 3 verdicts); **RED run 29905432689** for the stated reason; green again after revert. **Store migrated (harness #7, Car 3):** the 3 verdicts this proof counted lived in `docs/reviews/` at the time; that directory is retired and its bodies now live in `artifacts/reviews/` (`git mv`, history preserved) - the proof's run IDs and counts are historical and untouched, only the directory the verifier now defaults to has moved |
48	
49	**Deliberately not installed** (the retro's anti-autoimmune half): PSScriptAnalyzer - would
50	not have caught the encoding defects; shellcheck - two shell files; markdown linting -
51	ceremony with no incident behind it. Revisit when something earns them.
52	
53	**Still open from this retro:** the manual verdict-landing step (the harness train, #7),
54	the board's missing columns (#8), and the three guards parked on CI (#3, #4, #6) which were
55	deliberately NOT bundled into the first workflow.
56	
57	**Scorecard note for the next retro:** these three gates have caught nothing yet, because
58	they were installed today. If they are still catching nothing in a month, they are pruning
59	candidates - the Healing Loop counts catches per gate, and a gate that never fires is
60	ritual.

62	## 2026-07-22 - continued
63	
64	| When | Friction | Cost | Class |
65	|---|---|---|---|
66	| 07-22 | **Q4 ANSWERED BY LIVE TEST.** The fenced-block envelope survives the platform safety filter structurally - ` ```starcar-artifact ` lands intact - but angle brackets INSIDE the payload are HTML-escaped: `abstract: >` landed as `abstract: >`. A YAML block-scalar indicator is therefore unusable, and any `<placeholder>` syntax would be mangled too. The sentinel version was neutralised entirely; the fence version is mangled selectively, which is harder to notice. | One dispatch, and it answered a question rev 2 flagged as untested | Format constraint imposed by a layer we do not control |
67	| 07-22 | `subagent_tokens` is a context high-water mark, not a spend total. Reported 71,942 vs cache-creation sum 71,866 (0.1% match) vs **397,085** across all counters - a **5.52x** under-report on the smallest dispatch. Labelling it "an approximation" would have shipped a budget gauge reading ~18% of true burn. | Caught at design rung; would have been a shipped Law 1 defect on the fuel surface | A number that exists is not a number that means what you assumed |
68	| 07-22 | **CORRECTION, and the more important entry.** The row above originally read 365,867 / 5.1x. That figure came from deduplicating streaming usage records by taking the **first** record per `message.id`; the first partial of each turn carries `output_tokens: 1-4`, so it booked **23 output tokens for a dispatch that emitted 31,241** - a 99.93% loss of the exact quantity the finding is about. Last-per-id gives 397,085 / 5.52x. Verified both ways. | The institution's own record carried a corrupted number for ~20 minutes | Two dedup methods, neither named normative |
69	| 07-22 | **PROCESS VIOLATION, mine.** The reviewer measured 5.5x. I "independently verified" it, got 5.1x, and shipped my number in the design **without stating that I disagreed with the verdict I was answering**. `CLAUDE.md`: *"An implementer never silently overrides its own reviewer"* - rejection is appealable upward, never around. An appeal with evidence would have been legitimate AND would have been caught, because my evidence was wrong. Silence looked like agreement and hid a defect. | A wrong figure reached the design, the friction log, and a car's future cost derivation | Overriding a reviewer by silence rather than by appeal |

A prose habit produces prose-shaped evidence even when explicitly told to produce a test. | Round 4 | **Illustrating is not testing** |

The Healing Loop ("validated facts land as tests, never only prose") vs a protocol in prose. All three findable by looking; all three caught at review instead. | 3 dispatches' worth of findings that a lookup would have prevented | **The institution knew; the author did not consult it** |

Payload carries `agent_id`, `agent_transcript_path` (so the producer never scrapes the parent transcript - a Law 7 coupling removed), `last_assistant_message` and `background_tasks`. **But 70 of 74 have `agent_type: ""`** - internal harness subagents doing tool-level work, transcripts already deleted. This is precisely the prior art the missing-workflow class predicted we lacked, and it reveals three things the conductor has NOT been doing: fix cycles go to the SAME car (context intact) followed by a DELTA re-review scoped to verify-the-fix, not a fresh full reviewer every round; plans carry BINDING AMENDMENT BLOCKS that supersede stale task text; and reviewers may fault-inject locally provided they revert byte-identical. | **Five full fresh re-reviews at ~110k tokens each where deltas would have served** - the single largest waste of the session | Prior art existed and was not requested |

Wrong. **The prior art EXISTS in the ancestor shop; it was simply not ported so StarCar could access it.** That changes the remedy from "build from wreckage, slowly, driven by incidents" to "ASK". Aggravating: `docs/setup.md`'s trigger-gated table already says "port the ancestor's `session-start.sh` PATTERN" and "generalize from the ancestor shop's `run-suites` / `watch-ci` patterns" - read in the first ten minutes and parsed as *build later* rather than *prior art exists, ask for it*. | ~5 review rounds rediscovering written-down practice | **Prior art exists, unported, and nothing prompts the ask** |

The workflow behaved correctly - it went red rather than passing with zero tests. The conductor did not: I checked CI with `gh run list --limit 1`, frequently caught an `in_progress`, reported it, and never followed up. I said "CI green" repeatedly while a red sat in the history. This violates the repo's own rule - "verified means the pipeline that ships it went green" - and the ported `ops-script-patterns.md` §3 exists precisely to prevent it: "watch to completion, never triggered-probably-fine". **Found because the owner asked about a test count I could not reproduce.** | One unnoticed red; an hour of false "verified" claims | **Checked a status without waiting for a conclusion** |

Second cost in the same call: the previous invocation's cleanup had already deleted the probe fixture, so the retry also failed before the real fix. Two round-trips for one probe. | Two wasted probe runs | Bash-to-pwsh boundary mangles paths; known class, recurred |
91	| 07-22 | **Car 2's PowerShell stdin/pipeline traps** (reported by the car, logged by the conductor): (1) `$input` is EMPTY in any advanced script - a single `[Parameter()]`/`[ValidateSet]`/`[CmdletBinding]` silently disables top-level pipeline stdin, so the producer had to be a plain script with manual validation; (2) `$input` is a live enumerator consumed by any intervening statement - capture it as the FIRST executable line; (3) `git commit --only -- <path> -m msg` reads the message as a pathspec - `-m` must precede `--`; (4) array splat binds positionally and never interprets `-Name` tokens - named params need hashtable splat. | Cost absorbed inside one car dispatch; each found by a red, none shipped | The shell's implicit transformations are invisible until run - the probe doctrine's case, four more times |
92	| 07-22 | TWICE in one session the conductor answered an owner mid-turn question in text emitted between tool calls, where it did not surface - the owner had to ask "did my question get dropped?" both times ("what does this train produce"; "any design needed from my end"). The answers existed; the placement buried them. | Two owner round-trips to recover answers already written | Mid-turn text between tool calls is not a delivery surface; direct questions get answered at the START of the next end-of-turn message, always |

Working route: REST `POST /repos/{o}/{r}/pulls/{n}/requested_reviewers` with `reviewers[]=copilot-pull-request-reviewer[bot]`. | One probe, three observations, zero faith | Bot reviewers are invisible to the GraphQL login resolver; the REST endpoint is the mechanism |

The reviewer detected live mutation via file mtimes (16:54-16:57 against its 16:58 clock) and correctly quarantined its verdict to the frozen commit. | One review's isolation compromised; verdict salvaged because it pinned the SHA | One worktree = one actor at a time; reviews ALWAYS get their own detached worktree at the frozen SHA - the pattern existed and was skipped for speed |
95	| 07-22 | **RED CI MERGED AND CALLED DONE** (owner's sibling scar, paid here): PR-18 fixes merged to dev while BOTH CI legs were red on the index-staleness gate - the conductor never watched the run to completion (skipped the Handback-2 index reconcile, then claimed green). Caught by Copilot's second review pass, not by the conductor. | dev red on a public PR; a merge-to-main was one ruling away | Watched-to-terminal-and-recorded is the ONLY green; built scripts/Watch-CI.ps1 |
96	| 07-22 | **Watch-CI.ps1 caught THREE of its own bugs on first live test** before it could be trusted: (1) first-run-not-latest selection; (2) it grabbed Copilot's `dynamic` review run - a bot green masquerading as CI - at a SHA where both real CI legs failed; (3) a short-SHA input matched no full headSha and reported false-absence. All three found by running against real CI in both directions (must report red on red, must not report green on a bot run). | Nil - found in test, before any reliance | Tools that judge CI must be tested against real red AND real green, and against decoy runs |

The corrupted test broke parsing ("The term 'The' is not recognized"). Caught by the suite going red immediately. | One debug cycle | Backticks in an UNQUOTED heredoc are shell command substitution - quote the heredoc delimiter ('EOF') or avoid backticks when generating code via bash |
98	| 07-22 | **CONDUCTOR VIOLATED ITS OWN SOP, ONE ROUND AFTER CODIFYING IT.** The thread-provenance SOP (resolve every PR thread with a fix+SHA reply) was codified mid-PR-18 and honored on the first review round's 9 threads. Then Copilot's SECOND pass posted 9 more; the conductor FIXED all 9 in code (the correctness train) but never replied-and-resolved their threads. Caught by the OWNER looking at the PR, not by the conductor. | 9 threads left open with no evidence trail; owner had to catch it | Fixing the code is NOT closing the thread; DONE = the unresolved-thread query returns 0, checked before every merge proposal, never from memory |

100	## 2026-07-23
101	
102	| When | Friction | Cost | Class |
103	|---|---|---|---|
104	| 07-23 | **Environment parity gap between the conductor's two local shells**, surfaced on the FIRST use of the retro-#2 bash/pwsh discipline: the PowerShell tool's pwsh has no `sh` on PATH (`Get-Command sh` empty; Git's `usr/bin` absent from PATH), so `HookLatency.Probes.Tests.ps1:129` (`& sh $hook`) reds locally with CommandNotFoundException while CI is green on identical code at `58fbe23` and the Bash-tool env passes 12/12 (`/usr/bin/sh` present). Deterministic in one env, green in the other - an environment gap, NOT a flake and NOT a code defect. Triage cost one cycle; disposition: run probe suites from an env with `sh` on PATH; probe-hardening (resolve `sh` robustly) is a candidate only if this recurs. | One triage cycle before the first edit of the day | Local shell environments are not interchangeable; a suite baseline is only valid in the env that produced it |
105	| 07-23 | **GitNexus staleness marker did not advance after a successful reindex:** `node .gitnexus/run.cjs analyze` ran to completion (exit 0, ~07:55) yet every subsequent Bash hook still reports "last indexed: 307f120" across a dozen later commits. Either the analyze indexed without moving the marker the hook reads, or the hook caches. Cost so far: a recurring false-staleness nag per Bash call, tuned out - which is the crying-wolf failure mode the severity philosophy warns about. Not chased mid-train; triage trigger: next session start, or the first time an actual code-navigation query returns stale results. | Recurring noise; instrument credibility eroding | An instrument whose freshness marker lies after a successful run is worse than a stale index - the nag trains the operator to ignore it |
106	| 07-23 | **`git revert -q` RECURRED, same actor class, same session family:** the founding session logged this exact flag error (this log, 07-22 row 4); the conductor typed it again during the D10 red-injection cycle. This time the failure was LOUD (exit 129, usage text) rather than silent, so the cost was one retry - but a logged class recurring on the logger is the memory-evaporation thesis demonstrating itself. The durable fix is habit-level: revert = `git revert --no-edit` and nothing else. | One retry mid-handback | A logged instance does not immunize the logger; only mechanism or drilled habit does |
107	| 07-23 | **Entire checkpoint-mirror push failed transiently TWICE** (curl 65 `SEC_E_MESSAGE_ALTERED`, "unable to rewind rpc post data - try increasing http.postBuffer") during dev pushes at ~09:47 and ~10:19. The DEV push succeeded both times (verified by fetch+compare both times); only the transcript mirror lagged, and the hook re-syncs on the next push. Watch item: if the mirror falls persistently behind, the public-transcript invariant quietly degrades - check `entire/checkpoints/v1` freshness at session end. Candidate mechanical fix if it recurs: `git config http.postBuffer 524288000`. | Two verification cycles; no data loss | A mirroring hook that fails quietly-behind a successful push needs its own freshness check, or the invariant it serves rots invisibly |
108	| 07-23 | **BOARD-VISIBILITY GAP, owner-caught: 11 of 16 open issues were never linked to project 6.** Every conductor-created issue (#10-#21) satisfied the never-drop rule (durable issue exists) while silently bypassing the board - the owner's yard view was incomplete for a day+, discovered when #21 didn't appear. The meta-irony is the finding: this is precisely the lie-of-omission class StarCar exists to render loudly, proven on our own process. Instance fixed (all 11 added, re-audited to 21/21 visible); class fix: the project's built-in auto-add workflow (owner click, free, zero-maintenance) + a doctrine line in CLAUDE.md Tracking with a one-line audit command. | Owner operated on an incomplete board view for a day+ | An obligation satisfied at the ARTIFACT tier can still fail at the VISIBILITY tier; every surface a decision-maker reads needs its own completeness check |
109	| 07-23 | **Envelope escaping, third observation, first INSIDE a landed record:** the #20 reviewer (Opus) wrote its envelope fields as YAML block scalars (`findings: >`) - the exact form the mandate names as unusable - and the `>` landed HTML-escaped as `>` in the returned record's extracted text. The producer still parsed outcome/findings/abstract (record landed, integrity valid), so the cost is cosmetic contamination of two field values, not data loss. 1	// vocab.js - pure lookups into the wire's `vocabularies` block (positions,
2	// outcomes, roles, liveness - schema/yard-snapshot.schema.json's top-level
3	// `vocabularies` property). Recognition VALUES stay owned server-side by
4	// schema/vocab/*.json (Law 7); this module never hardcodes a taxonomy of
5	// its own - it only reads the defs the CURRENT snapshot already carried
6	// (design rev 5 S5.4 item 2 / Law 6: vocabularies travel on every snapshot
7	// so the view never fetches or hardcodes a separate copy that could drift).
8	//
9	// An id with no matching def is UNRECOGNISED - the discovery rule (design
10	// rev 5 S5.2, mockup brief "the discovery state") renders it hot, BY NAME,
11	// verbatim, never silently as calm.
12	
13	/**
14	 * @param {Array<{id: string, label: string, register: string}>} defs
15	 * @param {string} id
16	 */
17	export function findVocabDef(defs, id) {
18	  if (!Array.isArray(defs)) return undefined;
19	  return defs.find((d) => d && d.id === id);
20	}
21	
22	/**
23	 * Describe one vocabulary id against its defs array. Recognised ids get
24	 * their def's label + register; unrecognised ids render their raw id as
25	 * the label (verbatim) with register `needs-attention` (Law 1: unknown
26	 * must never look like "fine").
27	 *
28	 * @param {string} id
29	 * @param {Array<{id: string, label: string, register: string}>} defs
30	 */
31	export function describeVocab(id, defs) {
32	  const def = findVocabDef(defs, id);
33	  if (def) {
34	    return { id, label: def.label, register: def.register, recognised: true };
35	  }
36	  return { id, label: id, register: 'needs-attention', recognised: false };
37	}
38

1	# Car brief template
2	
3	Status: Current
4	
5	Every dispatch uses this shape. The framing rules are load-bearing: agents gradient
6	toward success shapes, so truth-telling must BE a success shape.
7	
8	```
9	You are Car <ID> on the <train> (repo: <repo>, <one-line project context>). You implement
10	<exact task list> from <the plan doc>. Work ONLY in the worktree at <path> on branch
11	<branch>. NEVER touch the shared checkout at <path>. Commit locally per task; NEVER push.
12	NO NESTED DELEGATION. <Platform notes: which suites run here, how.>
13	
14	FIRST verify base: `git log -1 --format='%H'` must show <sha> and the branch must be
15	<branch>. If not, STOP and report - do not proceed on a stale base.
16	
17	YOUR ORDERS: <plan doc path> - read <sections> IN FULL and execute in order, red-first
18	(write the failing test, RUN it, confirm it fails FOR THE STATED REASON - a red failing
19	for a different reason is a finding to report, not paper over). Ground truth on
20	ambiguity: <spec doc path>. Execute ONLY your tasks - <other cars' scopes> belong to
21	later cars; do not touch their files.
22	
23	<CROSS-CAR CONTEXT: what is already merged in the base, amendments that supersede stale
24	plan text, warnings from earlier cars' reviewers - verbatim, with file:line.>
25	
26	STANDING RULES binding every commit:
27	- DOCUMENTATION RANKS EQUAL TO CODE: every document your change invalidates - <name the
28	  ones this task plausibly touches: spec, ledger, gating matrix, setup doc, README,
29	  comments> - is updated in the SAME commit. A stale doc left behind is a Major finding
30	  at review, not a follow-up. Meat first, polish later.
31	- <state ledger / contracts rule with current baseline numbers and the STOP-if-different
32	  instruction>
33	- <lifecycle-test rule for new mutable state>
34	- The plan was verified against real code at <sha>, but if ANY snippet fails to compile
35	  or names a missing API, that is a plan defect: HONEST-STOP on that task with the exact
36	  error and file:line, continue with independent tasks. Honest stops are SUCCESS
37	  outcomes; improvising past a contradiction is the failure mode that costs trains.
38	- After your last task run <the full suite list with expected baselines> and build clean.
39	
40	FINAL REPORT: per task - commit SHA, red evidence (test name + observed failure reason),
41	green evidence (counts), deviations with justification; then total suite results, ledger
42	arithmetic as committed, findings/disclosures/honest stops. Your report feeds your
43	adversarial reviewer - make every claim verifiable.
44	
45	END YOUR REPORT WITH THE ARTIFACT ENVELOPE (mandatory - this is how your dispatch's
46	`returned` record gets its outcome; the producer hook extracts it from your transcript):
47	a fenced block, info string starcar-artifact, with three fields - outcome (done /
48	done-with-findings / honest-stop), findings, abstract. NO ANGLE BRACKETS anywhere inside
49	the envelope (they get HTML-escaped or filtered; the angle-bracket-free form lands clean).
50	```
51	
52	## Reviewer brief addendum
53	
54	```
55	You are the adversarial sentence-check reviewer for Car <ID>. Binding REJECT authority:
56	any Major = REJECT; disclosed-but-wrong does not clear review. READ-ONLY: edit nothing,
57	commit nothing, push nothing. Work ONLY in the detached worktree at <path> (HEAD must be
58	<sha> - verify first, STOP if not).
59	
60	SCOPE: <commits> against <plan/spec sections>.
61	VERIFY: <the specific claims from the car's report, each with how to check it>.
62	THE SENTENCE CHECK: <any cross-boundary value in this diff> - trace producer to final
63	consumer, every hop file:line, every hand-maintained mirror checked.
64	ADJUDICATIONS: <each disclosed deviation, to be ruled on against real code>.
65	RUN YOURSELF: <suites + expected counts>. Report observed.
66	DOC CHECK: <the documents this diff plausibly invalidates> - is each updated in the same
67	commit? Open every file:line the diff's docs cite and confirm the citation is true. A
68	stale document or a dead citation is a MAJOR finding; documents rank equal to code here.
69	DOC SENTENCE CHECK (if user-facing docs, or code they describe, are touched): <the claims
70	at issue> - trace each from prose to the command it names to the code that runs to what a
71	stranger observes, file:line at every hop, and state the trace. This is the PR-stage gate
72	for user-facing documentation; an untraceable claim is a finding.
73	GUARD CHECK: <any gate, guard, or protection this diff installs> - has anyone WATCHED it
74	fire? A config read-back or a passing-on-arrival test is an assertion, not an
75	observation; demand the fault-injection evidence or raise its absence as a finding.
76	CONVERGENCE CHECK (re-reviews only): <prior rounds' Major counts and the sections they
77	clustered in - the conductor MUST supply these; a fresh reviewer cannot know them>. Rule on
78	whether this series is converging, not only on whether this document is correct. If any two
79	of - Majors not declining, findings clustering in one section, or findings that are defects
80	the previous round's fixes created - then SET A CAP: name what the next revision must
81	demonstrate, and state that failing it escalates to the owner rather than to another round.
82	CONSTITUTION CHECK: name each law the diff implicates, one line of evidence each.
83	
84	VERDICT: APPROVE or REJECT up top; findings by severity with file:line.
85	
86	END YOUR REPORT WITH THE ARTIFACT ENVELOPE (mandatory - your verdict is a `returned`
87	dispatch too): a fenced block, info string starcar-artifact, fields outcome (APPROVE /
88	REJECT / honest-stop), findings, abstract. NO ANGLE BRACKETS inside the envelope.
89	```

content.text: Friction entry landed. Now dispatching Car 31 (Sonnet, per the standing model topology) into the existing clean worktree:

input.description: Car 31: register CSS fix
input.prompt:
You are Car 31 on the first-light iteration (repo: StarCar, the yard-board process showcase; the board is a Go server + vanilla-ESM browser view under board/). You implement the fix for issue #31: "First light: everything renders needs-attention red - per-item register resolution suspected broken." Work ONLY in the worktree at C:/Users/Chris/AppData/Local/Temp/claude/C--Users-Chris-git-starcar/67913b32-31ef-4c07-b6eb-71c56e7cedcc/scratchpad/worktrees/car-31 on branch car/31-register-css. NEVER touch the shared checkout at C:/Users/Chris/git/starcar. Commit locally per task; NEVER push. NO NESTED DELEGATION. Platform notes: node tests run from board/web with bare `node --test` (baseline 50/50 passing); Go is NOT on PATH in either tool shell - invoke it by full path `C:\Program Files\Go\bin\go.exe`.

FIRST verify base: `git log -1 --format='%H'` must show 38b67c8c0b9f75099aa334a5cf2af340c810920d and the branch must be car/31-register-css. If not, STOP and report - do not proceed on a stale base.

YOUR ORDERS (the conductor's diagnosis, already verified against the real captured snapshot - read these files in full before editing: board/web/css/board.css, board/web/js/dom-writer.js, board/web/js/render.js, board/web/js/compose.js):

DIAGNOSIS (verified): the JS pipeline is CORRECT - buildBoardViewModel against board/web/test/fixtures/real-snapshot.json computes every dispatch-row and car-chip stateRegister as "nominal" while the lanes themselves resolve "needs-attention" (from stale freshness, a separate issue #29). The all-red render is pure CSS: in board/web/css/board.css the rule `.register-nominal` declares ONLY `border-left-color` and never `color`, while `.register-in-progress` and `.register-needs-attention` declare both. dom-writer.js line 79 puts the lane-level register class on the lane container, whose `color` then CASCADES into every nominal child; and `.car-chip`, `.signal`, `.solari-row` draw their register edge with `border-left ... currentColor`, so even nominal items' borders pick up the inherited lane red. Issue #31 hypothesis (b) confirmed; hypotheses (a) and (c) ruled out (vocab ids match the wire exactly; per-item registers are computed per item).

TASK 1 (RED): create board/web/test/register-css.test.js pinning the CLASS of this defect: every register class must be COLOR-SELF-CONTAINED so no register can inherit another's color. The test: import REGISTER_ORDER from ../js/compose.js (never hardcode the register taxonomy - Law 7; compose.js is its single source in the view); read ../css/board.css (node:fs, URL-relative to the test file); for EACH register id assert a `.register-REGISTERID` rule block exists AND that the block declares BOTH `border-left-color` and `color`. Use plain string/regex extraction of each rule block - no CSS-parser dependency (the web view is deliberately dependency-free except the vendored validator). RUN `node --test` from board/web and CONFIRM it fails FOR THE STATED REASON: exactly the `.register-nominal` block missing a `color` declaration. A red failing for any different reason is a finding to report, not paper over. Commit the red test only after observing the red? NO - follow house TDD flow: observe the red, then implement Task 2, and commit test+fix together per task convention below.

TASK 2 (GREEN): add `color: var(--nominal);` to the `.register-nominal` rule in board/web/css/board.css. This is the whole fix - `--nominal` (#d9d5c9) equals `--text`, so nominal lanes look identical and nominal items inside a hot lane stop inheriting red; the currentColor borders on .car-chip/.signal/.solari-row recover their own register color for free. Do NOT remove `color` from the other two register rules (the lane header running hot is correct and intended), do NOT restructure dom-writer.js or render.js (out of scope - this is a coverage-class car), do NOT touch the freshness/IDLE behavior (that is issue #29, a design-moment decision reserved for the owner). Re-run `node --test`: all green, baseline 50 + your new passing test(s). Commit test + fix in ONE commit referencing #31.

STANDING RULES binding every commit:
- DOCUMENTATION RANKS EQUAL TO CODE: check the documents this change plausibly invalidates and confirm in your report you checked them: the CSS file's own comment block ("the three registers, and NOTHING else colors an element" - still true after the fix, confirm), docs/design/2026-07-23-ui-mockup-brief.md (three-register law - the fix implements it, should need no edit), README quickstart (untouched surface). If any is invalidated, update it in the SAME commit; a stale doc left behind is a Major finding at review.
- No state ledger entries are touched by this change (no mutable service state); if you find yourself touching one, that is a scope contradiction - HONEST-STOP with evidence.
- The diagnosis was verified against real code at 38b67c8. If ANY named file, rule, or line does not match what you find, that is a brief defect: HONEST-STOP on that task with the exact mismatch and file:line. Honest stops are SUCCESS outcomes; improvising past a contradiction is the failure mode that costs trains.
- After your last task run BOTH suites and report observed counts: (1) `node --test` from board/web - expect 50 baseline + your additions, zero failures; (2) `& 'C:\Program Files\Go\bin\go.exe' test ./...` from the board/ directory - expect 5/5 packages ok (your diff touches no Go, this is the collateral check). The Pester suite is deliberately NOT required for this car: the diff cannot touch scripts/ (state this skip in your report so it reads as a decision, not an omission).

FINAL REPORT: commit SHA; red evidence (test name + the observed failure message proving it failed for the stated reason); green evidence (observed counts both suites); the doc-check results; any deviations with justification; findings/disclosures/honest stops. Your report feeds your adversarial reviewer - make every claim verifiable.

END YOUR REPORT WITH THE ARTIFACT ENVELOPE (mandatory - this is how your dispatch's returned record gets its outcome; the producer hook extracts it from your transcript): a fenced block, info string starcar-artifact, with three fields - outcome (done / done-with-findings / honest-stop), findings, abstract. NO ANGLE BRACKETS anywhere inside the envelope (they get HTML-escaped or filtered; the angle-bracket-free form lands clean).

content.text:
Prep for #26 is done — the fix shape is clear: add `model` and `body_file` to `typedRecord` in `board/store/store.go:94` (which feeds the known-key set that raises the 52 `record-unrecognised-fields` conditions) and declare the same two fields in `schema/starcar-artifact.schema.json`, red-first on both sides. That brief is ready to write once the current unit lands.

Status while Car 31 runs, summarizing the session so far:

**Retro #4 (standing item):** Retro #3 ran earlier today and the friction log had nothing newer, so there was nothing to classify and nothing to install — the valid quiet outcome. CI baseline green on the last four `dev` commits. One new friction entry logged since: the checkpoint omitted a prepared-but-unused `car/31-register-css` branch+worktree from a prior session, which my `worktree add` collided with — a checkpoint-completeness class the drill's five claims didn't cover.

**#31 diagnosed — hypothesis (b), one line, exactly as the issue predicted.** The JS is correct: against the real captured snapshot, every dispatch row and car chip computes `stateRegister: "nominal"`; the lanes resolve `needs-attention` from stale freshness (that's #29, separate). The defect is CSS: `.register-nominal` declares only `border-left-color` and no `color` (`board.css:128`), so nominal items inherit the lane container's red — and `.car-chip`/`.signal`/`.solari-row` draw their edges with `currentColor`, spreading it to borders too.

**Car 31 dispatched** (Sonnet, per topology) into the pre-existing clean worktree: red-first stylesheet pin (every register class must be color-self-contained, taxonomy imported from `compose.js`, never hardcoded), then the one-line fix. Its Opus adversarial reviewer follows when it reports — cost line for the unit: one Sonnet car + one Opus review, small class.

**Housekeeping landed:** 18 open issues got their missing area label (tracking rule); 37 stale worktrees from the previous session and 11 fully-merged `car/*` branches pruned, per the checkpoint's sanction — only the live car-31 worktree remains.

I'll pick up automatically when the car reports.

<result>## Report — Car 31: issue #31 (register CSS fix)

**Base verified:** worktree HEAD was `38b67c8c0b9f75099aa334a5cf2af340c810920d` on branch `car/31-register-css` before any edit — matched the brief exactly.

**Commit:** `9c4730acaa02ce7d75ab7481d4829d916b7ec890` on `car/31-register-css` — "fix(#31): register-nominal must declare color, not just border-left-color". Contains both the new red test and the one-line CSS fix, per house TDD convention.

### Diagnosis confirmed against real code (all three brief citations checked and matched)
- `board/web/js/compose.js:13` — `REGISTER_ORDER = ['nominal', 'in-progress', 'needs-attention']`, imported by the test rather than hardcoded.
- `board/web/css/board.css:128-138` (pre-fix) — `.register-nominal` declared only `border-left-color`; `.register-in-progress` and `.register-needs-attention` declared both `border-left-color` and `color`.
- `board/web/js/dom-writer.js:79` — `` `lane lane-${lane.id} ${registerClass(lane.register)}` `` puts the lane's register class on the lane container, confirming the cascade path; lines 164, 175, 203 confirmed `border-left: 4px|3px solid currentColor` on `.car-chip`, `.signal`, `.solari-row`.

No mismatch found — no honest-stop needed on this task.

### Red evidence
`board/web/test/register-css.test.js`, isolated run before the fix (`node --test test/register-css.test.js`):
```
✖ .register-nominal is color-self-contained: declares BOTH border-left-color and color, so no register can inherit another's color (0.5145ms)
  AssertionError [ERR_ASSERTION]: .register-nominal must declare its own color (not just border-left-color) - otherwise this register's elements inherit color from an ancestor running a different register (issue #31)
      actual: '\n  border-left-color: var(--nominal);\n'
      expected: /(?<!-)\bcolor\s*:/
```
5 pass / 1 fail at that point — the sole failure is exactly the missing `color` declaration in `.register-nominal`, the stated reason. (`.register-nominal rule exists` and both `.register-in-progress`/`.register-needs-attention` color-self-contained checks passed, confirming the test isolates the defect correctly rather than failing broadly.)

### Fix
`board/web/css/board.css` — `.register-nominal` now reads:
```css
.register-nominal {
  border-left-color: var(--nominal);
  color: var(--nominal);
}
```

### Green evidence
- `node --test` from `board/web`: **56/56 passing, 0 failures** (50 baseline + 6 new: one "rule exists" + one "color-self-contained" test per register id, generated from `REGISTER_ORDER`).
- `C:\Program Files\Go\bin\go.exe test ./...` from `board/`: **5/5 packages ok** (`board`, `board/assemble`, `board/fold`, `board/server`, `board/store`) — collateral check, confirms no Go regression from a diff that touches no Go files.
- Pester suite: **deliberately skipped**, stated as a decision — this diff touches only `board/web/css/board.css` and `board/web/test/register-css.test.js`, never `scripts/`.

### Documentation check
- `board/web/css/board.css`'s own comment block (line 126: "the three registers, and NOTHING else colors an element") — still true: the fix adds `color: var(--nominal)`, reusing the existing nominal-register variable, not a fourth color. No edit needed.
- `docs/design/2026-07-23-ui-mockup-brief.md:43-46` ("the one hard aesthetic law: three severity colors, total... no fourth color") — read in full; still honored, no edit needed.
- Root `README.md` — grepped for `register`/`board.css`/`needs-attention`; zero matches. Confirmed untouched surface, no board-specific README exists under `board/web/`.

### Deviations
None. Scope held exactly to the brief: no edits to `dom-writer.js`, `render.js`, or freshness/IDLE behavior (#29, reserved for the owner).

### Note (not a blocker)
A PostToolUse hook flagged the GitNexus index as stale (last indexed `1182e09`) after the commit. This is a CSS/test-only change with no new/changed JS or Go symbols, so I did not run `gitnexus analyze` — flagging this as a disclosed decision rather than a silent skip, for the reviewer's awareness.

Added board/web/test/register-css.test.js, a red-first test importing REGISTER_ORDER from compose.js (never hardcoded) that extracts each ".register-REGISTERID" rule block from board.css by plain regex and asserts both border-left-color and color are declared - observed red for the stated reason (.register-nominal missing color) before the fix, green after. Committed both together at 9c4730acaa02ce7d75ab7481d4829d916b7ec890 on car/31-register-css, base 38b67c8c0b9f75099aa334a5cf2af340c810920d verified before editing. node --test (board/web): 56/56 passing (50 baseline + 6 new). go test ./... (board/): 5/5 packages ok, collateral, no Go touched. Pester suite explicitly skipped as a stated decision (diff never touches scripts/). Checked board.css's own comment claim and the mockup brief's three-register law; both still hold after the fix, no doc edits needed; root README has zero register/CSS references, confirmed untouched.

commit 9c4730acaa02ce7d75ab7481d4829d916b7ec890
Author: polecatspeaks <chris@polecatspeaks.com>
Date:   Thu Jul 23 16:40:52 2026 -0400

    fix(#31): register-nominal must declare color, not just border-left-color
    
    The all-red first-light render was pure CSS, not a JS defect: compose.js
    correctly resolves per-item stateRegister to nominal for every item in the
    real snapshot, but .register-nominal in board.css declared only
    border-left-color while .register-in-progress and .register-needs-attention
    declared both border-left-color AND color. dom-writer.js puts the lane-level
    register class on the lane container itself, so a hot lane's color cascaded
    into every nominal child; .car-chip/.signal/.solari-row draw their register
    edge with border-left: ... currentColor, so nominal items inherited the red
    border too.
    
    Fix: color: var(--nominal) added to .register-nominal. --nominal equals
    --text, so nominal elements look identical to plain text (as intended) and
    stop inheriting an ancestor's hot color.
    
    Red test (board/web/test/register-css.test.js) pins the class of the defect
    generally: every register class in REGISTER_ORDER (imported from compose.js,
    never hardcoded) must be color-self-contained, declaring both
    border-left-color and color, extracted from board.css by plain regex (no
    CSS-parser dependency, matching this view's dependency-free posture).

input.prompt:
You are the adversarial sentence-check reviewer for Car 31 on the StarCar first-light iteration (repo: StarCar, a process-showcase repo whose yard board is a Go server + vanilla-ESM browser view under board/). Binding REJECT authority: any Major = REJECT; disclosed-but-wrong does not clear review. READ-ONLY: edit nothing, commit nothing, push nothing. Work ONLY in the detached worktree at C:/Users/Chris/AppData/Local/Temp/claude/C--Users-Chris-git-starcar/e92f5a0b-1b9e-4913-98b3-c319b4d5e90b/scratchpad/review-car31 (HEAD must be 9c4730acaa02ce7d75ab7481d4829d916b7ec890 - verify first with git log -1 --format='%H', STOP if not). NEVER touch the shared checkout at C:/Users/Chris/git/starcar or the car's worktree. NO NESTED DELEGATION.

Platform notes: node tests run from board/web with bare `node --test`. Go is NOT on PATH in either tool shell - invoke it by full path `C:\Program Files\Go\bin\go.exe`.

SCOPE: the single commit 9c4730a (base 38b67c8c0b9f75099aa334a5cf2af340c810920d) against issue #31. Two files: board/web/css/board.css (one added line) and board/web/test/register-css.test.js (new, 59 lines).

CONTEXT - the issue and the conductor's diagnosis the car was handed:
Issue #31 ("First light: everything renders needs-attention red - per-item register resolution suspected broken") listed three hypotheses: (a) wire vocabularies lookup misses so the DETECTOR fires on everything; (b) a CSS register-class bug cascades the lane-level register into rows that carry their own; (c) the lane-level most-severe is applied per-item rather than per-lane-border. The conductor diagnosed (b), ruled out (a) and (c), and briefed the car to red-first a stylesheet pin and add `color: var(--nominal)` to `.register-nominal`.

VERIFY - each claim from the car's report, and re-derive it yourself, never by reading the report:
1. Base/commit claims: HEAD, branch lineage, and that the diff is exactly the two files above (no stray edits).
2. RED EVIDENCE: the car reports the new test failed for the stated reason before the fix - "AssertionError: .register-nominal must declare its own color", actual '\n  border-left-color: var(--nominal);\n', with 5 pass / 1 fail. You may FAULT-INJECT LOCALLY to re-derive this (revert the one CSS line, run the test, observe) PROVIDED you restore the file byte-identical afterwards and prove it (git status clean + git diff empty at the end). Confirm the red fails for THE STATED REASON and not incidentally.
3. NON-VACUITY: does the new test actually pin the class, or can it pass vacuously? Attack it: if board.css were empty or the rule renamed, does it fail? Does it truly import REGISTER_ORDER from compose.js rather than hardcoding a taxonomy (Law 7)? Does its regex have false-positive holes (e.g. would `border-left-color:` alone satisfy the `color:` assertion via a naive match - the car claims a lookbehind guards this; verify the guard works and that the test would catch a register rule that declares only `background-color` or `-webkit-text-fill-color`)?
4. GREEN EVIDENCE, RUN YOURSELF: `node --test` from board/web - expect 56/56 passing, 0 failures. `& 'C:\Program Files\Go\bin\go.exe' test ./...` from board/ - expect 5/5 packages ok. Report OBSERVED counts, never the expected ones.
5. THE ACTUAL FIX'S CORRECTNESS - this is the heart of the review, and the report is NOT evidence for it. The claim is that the all-red render is now resolved. Attack it independently: build the view model from the real captured snapshot (board/web/test/fixtures/real-snapshot.json) through board/web/js/render.js's buildBoardViewModel, and confirm per-item registers really are nominal (so hypothesis (a) is genuinely ruled out - check the vocabulary ids on the wire against schema/vocab/board-defs.json yourself). Then reason about the CSS cascade END TO END with the real class names dom-writer.js emits: does adding `color: var(--nominal)` to `.register-nominal` actually stop the inheritance for EVERY element that carries a register class, including (i) .car-chip and .signal and .solari-row whose border-left uses currentColor, (ii) the `car-outcome` span which carries its own registerClass INSIDE a chip, (iii) .board-condition items in the chrome, (iv) elements that carry NO register class at all but sit inside a hot lane (e.g. .car-subject, .solari-subject, .lane-title, .track-title, .yard-inventory-count) - do THOSE still inherit the lane's red, and if so is the fix incomplete for the issue as written ("everything renders needs-attention red")? Be precise about which elements are fixed and which are not. If children without a register class still inherit red from the lane container, say so plainly and rule on whether the issue is actually closed or only partly closed.
6. SCOPE DISCIPLINE: the brief forbade touching dom-writer.js, render.js, and freshness/IDLE behavior (#29 is an owner-reserved design moment). Confirm none were touched.

THE SENTENCE CHECK: the register value is a cross-boundary value. Trace it producer to final rendered pixel, every hop with file:line: the store record's outcome/state value -> the assembler/wire vocabularies block (board/assemble/) -> schema/vocab/board-defs.json's register assignment -> the browser's vocab.js describeVocab -> compose.js/render.js register resolution -> dom-writer.js's registerClass() -> the CSS rule in board.css -> what a human eye actually sees. Name every hop and state the trace in your verdict. Check every hand-maintained mirror for the register taxonomy (compose.js REGISTER_ORDER, the schema's $defs.register, board-defs.json, the CSS rules, any Go-side constant) and report whether they agree - a taxonomy that exists in five places is a Law 6 question worth ruling on.

DOC CHECK: the documents this diff plausibly invalidates - board/web/css/board.css's own comment block (line ~126, "the three registers, and NOTHING else colors an element"), docs/design/2026-07-23-ui-mockup-brief.md (the three-severity-colors law), and any design/spec section describing register rendering (grep docs/design/ and docs/specs/ for register). Is each still true, and is each updated in the same commit where it is not? Open every file:line the diff's comments and commit message cite and confirm the citation is TRUE - a dead or drifted citation is a MAJOR finding; documents rank equal to code here.

GUARD CHECK: the new test is a guard. Has anyone WATCHED it fire? The car claims it did. Demand and re-derive the fault-injection evidence yourself (item 2 above) - a passing-on-arrival test is an assertion, not an observation.

DISCLOSED DEVIATION TO ADJUDICATE: the car disclosed that it did NOT re-run GitNexus analyze after committing (index reported stale at 1182e09), reasoning that a CSS/test-only change adds no new symbols. Rule on whether that reasoning holds.

CONSTITUTION CHECK: name each law this diff implicates with one line of evidence each. At minimum consider Law 1 (a confident falsehood on an information surface - the board was showing red for calm work, which is exactly a Law 1 defect on the shop's own truth surface; does the fix fully retire it?), Law 7 (no hardcoded taxonomies - the test importing REGISTER_ORDER), Law 6 (second copies of the register taxonomy), and the TDD rule (red-first, non-vacuous).

CONVERGENCE CHECK: not applicable - this is round 1 of this car, no prior rounds exist.

VERDICT: APPROVE or REJECT up top; findings by severity (Major/Minor/Note) with file:line each. Remember the house rule: REJECT is a SUCCESS outcome, effort is never a mitigating factor, and softening a finding to be polite produces a spelling check. Equally, do not manufacture a finding to look thorough.

END YOUR REPORT WITH THE ARTIFACT ENVELOPE (mandatory - your verdict is a returned dispatch too): a fenced block, info string starcar-artifact, with three fields - outcome (APPROVE / REJECT / honest-stop), findings, abstract. NO ANGLE BRACKETS anywhere inside the envelope (they get HTML-escaped or filtered; the angle-bracket-free form lands clean).

116	// parallel list that could drift from it (Law 6).
117	var typedKeys = buildTypedKeys()
118	
119	func buildTypedKeys() map[string]bool {
120		keys := map[string]bool{}
121		t := reflect.TypeOf(typedRecord{})
122		for i := 0; i < t.NumField(); i++ {
123			tag := t.Field(i).Tag.Get("json")
124			name := strings.Split(tag, ",")[0]
125			if name != "" {
126				keys[name] = true
127			}
128		}
129		return keys
130	}
131	
132	// Scan walks storeRoot recursively for *.json files (artifacts/**/*.json),
133	// double-decodes each one, and returns every record that survives, every one
134	// quarantined, and the board conditions the scan itself raised. now is the
135	// injected clock (never wall-clock read directly here - board/server's poll
136	// loop is the one place "now" is sampled), used only to detect a
137	// future-dated "at".
138	//
139	// The returned error is reserved for a SCAN-LEVEL failure (the directory
140	// itself missing or unreadable, design S6 row 1: "Lane failed, coded reason,
141	// lastGood visibly marked"). A directory that EXISTS but holds zero records
142	// is a SUCCESSFUL scan (DR3-5a, honest-empty) - it returns a nil error and
143	// an empty ScanResult, never conflated with the failure row above it.
144	func (a *Adapter) Scan(storeRoot string, now time.Time) (ScanResult, error) {
145		info, err := os.Stat(storeRoot)
146		if err != nil {
147			return ScanResult{}, fmt.Errorf("store: %s is missing or unreadable: %w", storeRoot, err)
148		}

input.prompt:
You are Car 26 on the first-light iteration (repo: StarCar, a process-showcase repo; the yard board is a Go server + vanilla-ESM browser view under board/, the artifact store is artifacts/**/*.json written by PowerShell producer scripts under scripts/). You implement issue #26. Work ONLY in the worktree at C:/Users/Chris/AppData/Local/Temp/claude/C--Users-Chris-git-starcar/e92f5a0b-1b9e-4913-98b3-c319b4d5e90b/scratchpad/car26 on branch car/26-schema-drift. NEVER touch the shared checkout at C:/Users/Chris/git/starcar, and never touch any other worktree. Commit locally per task; NEVER push. NO NESTED DELEGATION.

Platform notes: Go is NOT on PATH in either tool shell - invoke it by full path `C:\Program Files\Go\bin\go.exe`. Pester runs via pwsh. Node tests run from board/web with bare `node --test`.

FIRST verify base: `git log -1 --format='%H'` must show 38b67c8c0b9f75099aa334a5cf2af340c810920d and the branch must be car/26-schema-drift. If not, STOP and report - do not proceed on a stale base.

THE PROBLEM (issue #26, verbatim disposition): the board's first live serve disclosed that 52 of the store's records carry fields the schema never enumerated - `model` (27 records) and `body_file` (25 records). The board raised 52 `record-unrecognised-fields` board conditions. That disclosure is Law 1/Law 4 working exactly as designed - nothing was suppressed. But both fields are OBSERVED PRODUCER FIELDS WITH CLEAR PROVENANCE, so declaring them follows the same epistemic rule as the outcome-vocabulary additions: an observed, provenanced producer field gets DECLARED, not suppressed. Until reconciled the 52 conditions are honest but noisy, which is the crying-wolf calibration problem (expected patterns are NOTES, defects are FLAGS).

PROVENANCE, verified by the conductor at this base - open each and confirm before relying on it; a mismatch is a brief defect and an HONEST-STOP:
- `model`: written at scripts/Produce-Artifact.ps1:285, guarded to `kind -eq 'dispatched'` only, sourced from the Task tool_response's `resolvedModel` (read at scripts/Produce-Artifact.ps1:204). Example value: claude-sonnet-5.
- `body_file`: written at scripts/Migrate-Verdicts.ps1:152, on migrated verdict records; the script's own header comment at scripts/Migrate-Verdicts.ps1:22 already calls it "an open-posture extra".
- The unknown-field diff that raises the condition: board/store/store.go - `typedRecord` (line ~94) is the KNOWN field set, reflected into `typedKeys` by `buildTypedKeys()` (line ~119) via json tags, and the condition is raised with code `record-unrecognised-fields` at store.go:294.
- NOTE, load-bearing: schema/starcar-artifact.schema.json does NOT set additionalProperties:false, so the SCHEMA is not what rejects these fields - the board's typedRecord known-key diff is. Both surfaces still need updating, for different reasons: the schema because it is the written contract a stranger reads, the struct because it is the mechanism that fires.

YOUR ORDERS - two tasks, each red-first (write the failing test, RUN it, confirm it fails FOR THE STATED REASON; a red failing for a different reason is a finding to report, never to paper over):

TASK 1 (Go side, the mechanism): red-first in board/store/store_test.go - a test asserting that a record carrying `model` and `body_file` raises NO `record-unrecognised-fields` condition. Follow the existing test file's conventions exactly (read it in full first; reuse its fixture/helper patterns rather than inventing new ones). RUN it, observe the red naming those two fields as unrecognised. Then add the two fields to `typedRecord` with correct json tags (`model`, `body_file`) and types matching what the producers actually write (both are strings - verify against the real records in artifacts/ before choosing the type). Place them with a comment explaining WHY they are declared (the observed-producer-field rule above), never WHAT they are. Green: `& 'C:\Program Files\Go\bin\go.exe' test ./...` from board/ - expect 5/5 packages ok.

TASK 2 (schema side, the contract): declare `model` and `body_file` in schema/starcar-artifact.schema.json's properties, each with a description carrying its PROVENANCE (which script writes it, under what condition) in the style of the neighbouring property descriptions - read them first and match their voice; `producer` and `cost` are the closest models since both are optional producer-written extras. Neither field is required. If a schema-conformance test or vector exists that should pin this, add the red for it first; if none exists, state in your report that the schema half is contract-only with no executable pin and say why (do NOT invent a test framework that is not already in this repo - check what exists first).

DO NOT: suppress, filter, or downgrade the `record-unrecognised-fields` condition mechanism itself (that is the honest detector and it must keep firing for genuinely unknown fields - removing or weakening it would be a Law 1/Law 4 violation and an instant Major). DO NOT touch the board's view layer (board/web/), the freshness/IDLE behavior (#29, owner-reserved), or the condition-grouping question (#30, separate issue). DO NOT edit any file under artifacts/ - store records are landed records; rewriting one is tampering with the record.

VERIFY THE OUTCOME EMPIRICALLY (this is the point of the change, and the report is not evidence): after the fix, re-derive that the 52 conditions are actually gone. Run the board's store scan against the REAL store (artifacts/) and count the remaining `record-unrecognised-fields` conditions - a Go test, a tiny throwaway Go program under the worktree's own scratch, or the server's own snapshot path, whichever the existing code makes cleanest. Report the observed before-count and after-count. If the after-count is not zero, report the remaining field names verbatim - that is a discovery, not a failure.

STANDING RULES binding every commit:
- DOCUMENTATION RANKS EQUAL TO CODE: every document this change invalidates is updated in the SAME commit. Candidates to CHECK (grep, do not assume): the typedRecord comment block in store.go (it explains the known-key set and cites DR3-1 - is its story still true with two more fields?), schema/starcar-artifact.schema.json's own $comment blocks if any, docs/specs/ and docs/design/ sections describing the artifact record's field set or the D17 unknown-field disclosure, docs/glossary.md if it defines the record fields, and any state ledger. A stale doc left behind is a Major finding at review. Meat first, polish later.
- Open every file:line you cite in a comment or commit message and confirm the citation is TRUE. A dead citation is a Major.
- The brief was verified against real code at 38b67c8, but if ANY named file, line, or API does not match what you find, that is a plan defect: HONEST-STOP on that task with the exact error and file:line, and continue with independent work. Honest stops are SUCCESS outcomes; improvising past a contradiction is the failure mode that costs trains.
- After your last task run the FULL suite set and report OBSERVED counts: (1) `& 'C:\Program Files\Go\bin\go.exe' test ./...` from board/ - baseline 5/5 packages ok; (2) `node --test` from board/web - baseline 50/50 passing (your diff should not touch it; this is the collateral check); (3) the Pester suite - baseline 247/247 at this base; run it and report observed, noting that scripts/probes may red in a shell without `sh` on PATH (a KNOWN environment gap, logged in docs/friction-log.md, NOT a defect in your change - if you see it, name it as that known gap rather than reporting a regression).

FINAL REPORT: per task - commit SHA, red evidence (test name + the observed failure message proving it failed for the stated reason), green evidence (observed counts), the before/after condition counts against the real store, doc-check results, deviations with justification, findings/disclosures/honest stops. Your report feeds your adversarial reviewer - make every claim verifiable.

END YOUR REPORT WITH THE ARTIFACT ENVELOPE (mandatory - this is how your dispatch's returned record gets its outcome; the producer hook extracts it from your transcript): a fenced block, info string starcar-artifact, with three fields - outcome (done / done-with-findings / honest-stop), findings, abstract. NO ANGLE BRACKETS anywhere inside the envelope (they get HTML-escaped or filtered; the angle-bracket-free form lands clean).

content.text:
Two dispatches are in flight. Here's where things stand.

**#31 is fixed and under adversarial review.** Car 31 (Sonnet) landed `9c4730a` on `car/31-register-css`: a red-first stylesheet pin plus the one-line fix. It confirmed the diagnosis at every cited line and reported the red failing for exactly the stated reason (`.register-nominal` missing a `color` declaration), then 56/56 node tests and 5/5 Go packages green. The test imports the register taxonomy from `compose.js` rather than hardcoding it, so it pins the *class* — any future register that declares a border color without its own text color reds by name.

The Opus reviewer is now running in its own detached worktree at the frozen SHA. I gave it one attack the car was not asked to consider: whether the fix actually closes the issue *as written*. Elements carrying a register class are demonstrably fixed, but children with no register class of their own — `.car-subject`, `.solari-subject`, `.lane-title` — still sit inside a lane container that sets `color` when hot. If they still inherit red, the issue is partly closed, not closed, and I'd rather the gate tell me that than discover it at second light.

**#26 dispatched in parallel** (Sonnet, own worktree, independent files). This is the 52 noisy `record-unrecognised-fields` conditions. Worth flagging what I found while writing the brief: the schema does *not* set `additionalProperties: false`, so the schema was never what rejected these fields — the board's own `typedRecord` known-key diff in `board/store/store.go:94` is. Both surfaces need the two fields for different reasons, and the brief says so explicitly rather than letting the car assume one fix covers both. Both fields have traced provenance (`model` from `Produce-Artifact.ps1:285`, `body_file` from `Migrate-Verdicts.ps1:152`). The car must re-derive the before/after condition count against the real store; the report is not evidence that the noise is gone.

**Session housekeeping done:** the tooling retro was quiet (Retro #3 ran earlier today, nothing newer in the log) and installed nothing, which is the valid anti-autoimmune outcome. CI baseline is green on the last four `dev` commits. I logged one new friction entry: the checkpoint omitted a prepared-but-unused branch and worktree from a prior session, which my `worktree add` collided with — the drill's five graded claims didn't cover prepared-but-unlanded state, so that's a finding about the checkpoint template, not about the drill. I also gave 18 open issues their missing area label and pruned 37 stale worktrees plus 11 fully-merged car branches.

Nothing has been pushed and nothing has reached `dev`. I'll report both verdicts when they land.

re: the drill. The drill itself worked, however I got a suprise drill from the universe, I had to up my monthly credits usage, so the agent rightfully stopped where it was. So I took that as a good chance to run another unannounced surprise drill. CHaos monkey the process.
That reframes everything — and it means my first finding of the session was wrong. Let me verify the forensics before I assert anything.

=== producer launch hook ===
#!/bin/sh
# starcar-producer-launch.sh -- the PostToolUse:Task producer hook (spec S2.1). Claude Code
# feeds the launch payload on stdin; this forwards it to the ONE writer, which emits a
# `dispatched` record. The record's `subject` is tool_response.agentId, which equals the
# stop payload's agent_id for the same dispatch (Probe 5, measured), so subject identity
# holds end to end across the two hooks with no remembered state.
#
# NON-FATAL BY DESIGN: pwsh missing means no record this run (exit 0), never a broken
# dispatch. When pwsh is present the producer's own exit code flows (raise, never drop).
DIR="${CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR:-.}"
if ! command -v pwsh >/dev/null 2>&1; then exit 0; fi
exec pwsh -NoProfile -File "$DIR/scripts/Produce-Artifact.ps1" -Kind dispatched

#!/bin/sh
# SessionStart hook: announce a /goodnight resume packet if one exists.
# Written by the goodnight skill (.claude/skills/goodnight/SKILL.md) when a session
# closes with agents still in flight. Background agents die with their session, so the
# packet is a re-dispatch spec the next session must read FIRST.
packet="$HOME/.claude/projects/C--Users-Chris-git-starcar/memory/resume-packet.md"
if [ -f "$packet" ]; then
  echo "[goodnight] RESUME PACKET FOUND from the previous session: $packet"
  echo "[goodnight] Unfinished work is recorded there. Read it FIRST, fold it into the"
  echo "[goodnight] working-state memory, re-dispatch what it names, then DELETE the packet."
fi
exit 0

**This checkpoint is written CONTINUOUSLY, not at session end.** A session can die
without warning (it did today: a credit limit killed session `67913b32` mid-work at
~16:21). A checkpoint that is only written at a graceful close does not exist when the
close is not graceful. Update this file the moment state changes — especially on dispatch.

## THE LIVE DEFECT FOUND THIS SESSION (highest-value open thread)
**The producer hook silently skipped 2 of this session's 3 dispatches.** Only Car 26
(`a3bfb5c078bd8da96`) got a `dispatched` record; Car 31 and its reviewer got none, and
`git status` is clean so they were never written (not written-and-uncommitted, which is
the OTHER known failure mode — see `1182e09`). All day before this the producer was
regular: 20 dispatched records, ~10:14 through 15:00.

**Why this is structural, not cosmetic:** the harness detects a missing artifact by
finding a `dispatched` record with no matching `returned` one. A dispatch that never
writes a `dispatched` record is not overdue — it is INVISIBLE. The mechanism built to
make a killed session survivable has a blind spot exactly where the kill happens.
Root cause NOT yet established (hypothesis, unproven: git index contention with the
conductor's own concurrent heavy git operations right after dispatch). Do not assert a
cause without probing it.

---
name: chaos-monkey-drills-are-unannounced
description: The owner runs unannounced chaos-monkey drills; any anomaly may be a deliberate test, and the checkpoint must survive a kill that gives no warning.
metadata:
  type: feedback
---

The owner runs **unannounced chaos-monkey drills** on the process (owner coinage in this
repo, 2026-07-23). The #21 drill series has a planned, gentle variant; this is the
opposite — real disruption, no announcement, and the owner will take an accident of the
universe (a credit limit killing a session mid-work) and deliberately convert it into a
second drill rather than smoothing it over.

**Why:** the planned drill grades the checkpoint against a *graceful* handoff, which is
the easy case and the one the process was designed for. A kill that arrives without
warning tests the thing that actually matters — whether state was durable at the instant
of death, not whether a tidy close was written afterwards. The owner is deliberately
attacking the premise that sessions end when the conductor decides they end.

**How to apply:**
- Treat an unexplained anomaly (a branch nobody remembers cutting, a worktree with no
  commits, a checkpoint describing a future that already happened) as possible evidence
  of a killed session, and STOP on the contradiction rather than inventing a benign
  story that lets work proceed. Improvising past a contradiction is a named failure.
- Write the checkpoint CONTINUOUSLY, on every state change, especially on dispatch —
  never as a session-end ritual. See [[owner-sleeps-during-long-runs]]: same class,
  different trigger.
- Grade honestly and report the misses first. A drill the conductor "passes" by
  rationalising is worth nothing. REJECT and self-disclosure are success outcomes here
  ([[agreeableness-is-my-failure-mode]]).
- The debris of a killed session is forensic evidence: reflog timestamps, worktree paths
  carrying the dead session's id, and the artifact store's `dispatched`-without-`returned`
  records all date the death and bound what was lost.

cd C:\Users\Chris\git\starcar
$b1 = @'
Producer hook silently wrote NO dispatched record for 2 of 3 dispatches (invisible, not overdue)

OBSERVED 2026-07-23 (session e92f5a0b), found by hand during forensics on a killed session, NOT by any instrument.

Three dispatches were made this session. Only one landed a `dispatched` record:
- Car 26 `a3bfb5c078bd8da96` -> artifacts/.../dispatched-20260723T204354Z.json, auto-committed as 0667521. GOOD.
- Car 31 `addbb80a3b64cbf2e` (~16:38) -> NO RECORD.
- Car 31's Opus reviewer `ad2b8215ab13183c4` (~16:42) -> NO RECORD.

`git status` was clean throughout, so the two records were never WRITTEN. That is distinct from the other known failure mode (producer writes, record sits untracked and unnoticed - see 1182e09, caught a day late by an owner-ordered sweep).

Not a chronic fault: all day prior the producer was regular, 20 dispatched records between 10:14 and 15:00 with matching `harness:` auto-commits.

WHY THIS IS STRUCTURAL, NOT COSMETIC
The harness detects a missing artifact by finding a `dispatched` record with no matching `returned` one (overdue / presumed-lost). A dispatch that never writes its `dispatched` record is therefore not overdue - it is INVISIBLE. The detector cannot see its own producer's silence. This is absence-blindness one layer below where the design placed its guard, and it is blind in exactly the scenario the mechanism exists for: a session that dies mid-flight leaving no other trace.

ROOT CAUSE NOT ESTABLISHED. Hypothesis only, explicitly UNPROVEN and not to be repeated as fact: git index contention, since the conductor ran 37 `git worktree remove` plus 11 `git branch -d` immediately after the Car 31 dispatch, while the hook auto-commits. The reviewer dispatch had no such concurrent git activity, which weakens that hypothesis and is itself a reason to probe rather than assume.

WHAT WOULD CLOSE THIS
1. PROBE FIRST (behavioural claim - must be RUN, never reasoned): instrument `scripts/Produce-Artifact.ps1` / the launch hook to record every invocation and its exit path, then dispatch repeatedly under concurrent git load and observe. Reading the script cannot settle this.
2. Then a mechanism, not vigilance: the hook is currently "NON-FATAL BY DESIGN" (exit 0 when pwsh is missing), which is correct for not breaking a dispatch but means every other silent failure is also swallowed. A dispatch-count reconcile (conductor-observed dispatches vs records landed) would make the silence loud.
3. Consider whether the detector can ever see this class, or whether a second independent witness is required by construction.

cd C:\Users\Chris\git\starcar
$b2 = @'
OWNER QUESTION, 2026-07-23: "since we're doing web based for starcar... do we need a framework like playwright?"

Filed under never-drop rather than answered in conversation. Retro rule 5 check done FIRST: `docs/setup.md`'s trigger-gated table has NO browser/visual-testing row, and the repo has zero mentions of playwright/puppeteer/jsdom/headless. So this is a genuine gap, not a deliberate deferral being misread as one.

THE EVIDENCE THAT THE TRIGGER HAS ALREADY FIRED

1. Issue #31 (the board rendering every row needs-attention red) passed ALL 50 node tests. The view model was provably correct - per-item registers all resolved nominal against the real captured snapshot. The defect lived entirely in the CSS cascade: `.register-nominal` set `border-left-color` but not `color`, so nominal children inherited a hot lane's color, and `.car-chip`/`.signal`/`.solari-row` draw their edge with `currentColor`. `board/web/test/minidom.js` is a hand-rolled DOM shim with NO CSS engine, so the entire class of computed-style/cascade defects is structurally invisible to the current suite. It was caught by a human eye at first light.

2. #31's own fix does not guard #31's class. The pin (`board/web/test/register-css.test.js`) is a REGEX OVER THE STYLESHEET'S TEXT. It asserts each `.register-*` rule declares both `border-left-color` and `color`. That pins the file's content; it proves nothing about a rendered pixel. Add `.lane { color: red }` tomorrow and the regex test stays green while the board goes all-red again. A guard that cannot fail for the defect it was written for is the decorative-guard class this repo already has a scar for (branch protection, `enforce_admins: false`).

3. The near-term backlog is almost entirely visual: #30 (conditions grouping / severity calibration, explicitly a HAVAGLANCE question), #29 (IDLE is a state), #12 (car health bar). "Does it render right" is about to be the binding question repeatedly, and we currently have no mechanical way to answer it.

4. `CLAUDE.md` names screenshot drift as one of two surfaces left unguarded even after CI lands, and calls it "both the likeliest and the most embarrassing rot this repo can ship" for a status-board project. A browser driver regenerates `docs/screenshots/` mechanically.

5. FULL SENTENCE FLOW currently ends at a human. The register value crosses store record -> Go assembler -> wire vocabularies -> SSE -> browser JS -> CSS rule -> what an eye sees. Every hop but the last is machine-checked. `getComputedStyle(el).color` makes the last hop empirical - which is the probe doctrine's own rule, since a cascade outcome is a BEHAVIOURAL claim and the doctrine says behavioural claims are settled only by RUNNING.

WHY A REAL BROWSER SPECIFICALLY, NOT A LIGHTER SHIM
jsdom and happy-dom have incomplete cascade and computed-style support. For this exact class they would be a LYING instrument, and this shop's severity philosophy holds that an instrument that cries wolf (or falsely reassures) is worse than none. That disqualifies them on doctrine, not on taste. A real engine is the only honest instrument for a cascade question.

WHAT WE ALREADY HAVE, AND WHY IT IS NOT ENOUGH
The Claude-in-Chrome browser tools are already listed in `docs/setup.md` as ready-now, and are how first light happened. But they are conductor-driven, not runnable in CI, and produce a perishable observation. The probe doctrine is explicit: a probe becomes substrate only when it LANDS as a pinned test. We have the probe half and not the landing half. Chrome tools = the drill; playwright = the pinned red.

COSTS, HONESTLY
- Browser binaries are hundreds of MB; CI gets slower and needs a cache step.
- It would be the first npm-installed dev dependency in a view that is deliberately dependency-free. Precedent exists and points the right way: `docs/setup.md` already reasons that D19's no-build-step rule "binds the BROWSER, never Node - Node is what RUNS the tests". A test-toolchain dependency is not a shipped dependency.
- Cross-platform is fine (issue #14 portability, ubuntu CI leg) but must be verified, not assumed.

RIGHT-SIZED FIRST BITE (NIRTS, not a suite)
ONE spec: serve the real board from the real store, assert `getComputedStyle` colour for a nominal row inside a hot lane (the exact #31 defect, as a real red first), plus one assertion per register, plus mechanical regeneration of the first-light screenshot. Not a broad E2E suite, not page-object scaffolding.

BEFORE BUILDING: ASK FOR THE PRIOR ART. Does the ancestor shop have a browser/visual test pattern to port? The scar for not asking was five design rounds rediscovering written-down practice. The question costs one sentence and belongs to the owner.

DECISION OWNER: the owner (CI minutes, machine config, dependency posture). Trigger stated: the next visual ticket (#30 or #29) is the natural moment.
'@
