CI proven in both directions, and six public verdicts. Not consolation - accounting.*

## WHEN REALITY DOES NOT MEET SPEC: the human adjudicates (owner ruling, 2026-07-23)

Sooner or later a contract, a spec, or a written posture will not fit the thing in front of
you. **That is not yours to resolve.** Kick it up to the human in the loop for final
adjudication - and understand that the adjudication may SHARPEN the definition or LOOSEN
it, because both are legitimate outcomes. It is decided **case by case: never assumed,
never inferred, never projected from the agent's end.**

The two failure modes are mirror images, and both are the agent quietly taking the
owner's seat:

- **Reading the LETTER and stopping.** "The doc says no npm install, so this is
  forbidden." That treats a document as a fence with no reason behind it - the exact
  Chesterton's-fence error this file exists to prevent.
- **Deciding the SPIRIT is met and proceeding.** "It never ships, so it doesn't really
  count as a dependency." Even when that reasoning is *correct*, an agent reaching it
  alone has adjudicated a contract it does not own.

The tell that you are in this situation rather than an ordinary one: **you are about to
explain why a written rule does not really apply here.** That sentence is the trigger.
Stop and escalate on it.

This is the same instinct as the standing vocabulary rule (PAUSE AND ASK, never infer),
the honest stop (a car hitting a plan-vs-code contradiction), and the swirl-and-churn
escalation - one family, one reflex, at four different rungs. The unifying principle:
**an agent may report a contradiction, never resolve one it does not own.**

*Scar, and it is a near-miss rather than a cost, which is the only reason it is cheap.
Issue #33 adopted Playwright. Probing found the repo had no `package.json` anywhere and
that `docs/setup.md` claimed "not a browser dependency ... no npm install, no bundler" -
an apparent posture reversal. The conductor did flag it to the owner. It ALSO, in the same
breath, wrote its own answer into the dispatched car brief as settled fact ("THIS COMMIT
MAKES BOTH CLAIMS FALSE"), which is projection: the question was escalated and
simultaneously pre-answered, so the escalation was decorative. The owner then ruled the
opposite way - the split is SHIPPED RUNTIME versus TEST TOOLCHAIN, the install is
gate-triggered and legitimate, and the spirit of the contract is met if not the letter.
Aggravating: `docs/setup.md` ALREADY drew that exact line one sentence away ("D19's
no-build-step rule binds the BROWSER ... never Node - Node is what RUNS the tests"), so
the answer was on the page and the conductor projected past it. Caught only because the
owner was present and fast; had the ruling come an hour later, a car would have written
the conductor's projection into the repo's own posture documentation as truth.*

## Review calibration (binding, uniform)

When reality does not meet spec, **the human adjudicates.** The owner may SHARPEN the
definition or LOOSEN it; both are legitimate. It is case by case - **never assumed, never
inferred, never projected from my end** (owner ruling, 2026-07-23).

**Why:** the two ways I get this wrong are mirror images, and both are me quietly taking
the owner's seat. Reading the LETTER and stopping ("the doc forbids this") treats a
document as a fence with no reason behind it. Deciding the SPIRIT is met and proceeding
("it never ships, so it doesn't count") is worse, because it feels like good judgment -
and even when the reasoning is right, I have adjudicated a contract I do not own.

**The tell:** I am about to explain why a written rule does not really apply here. That
sentence is the trigger. Stop and escalate on it.

**How to apply:**
- Escalate the question WITHOUT pre-answering it. Flagging a contradiction to the owner
  while simultaneously writing my own answer into a dispatched brief is projection, and it
  makes the escalation decorative - the car ships my guess as fact before the ruling lands.
- Check whether the document already answers it. In the #33 case `docs/setup.md` drew the
  shipped-versus-toolchain line one sentence away from the claim I thought was violated.
- Record the ruling durably with its reasoning, so the next reader does not re-stumble.
- Same family, different rungs: [[ask-dont-infer-owner-vocabulary]] (vocabulary), the
  honest stop (plan-vs-code), swirl-and-churn (review rounds). One reflex: **report a
  contradiction, never resolve one I do not own.**

a fence with no reason behind it), and deciding the SPIRIT is met and proceeding (which
feels like good judgment and is worse, because even correct reasoning reached alone has
adjudicated a contract the agent does not own).

The tell is written down because it is checkable in the moment: you are about to explain
why a written rule does not really apply here. That sentence is the trigger.

Named as one family with three rules already in this file - the vocabulary rule (PAUSE
AND ASK), the honest stop (plan-vs-code), and swirl-and-churn escalation - at four
different rungs, under one principle: an agent may REPORT a contradiction, never RESOLVE
one it does not own. Cross-referenced rather than restated, per the retro's dedup
discipline.

Scar attached, and it is mine: on #33 the conductor DID flag the apparent posture
reversal to the owner, and in the same breath wrote its own answer into the dispatched
car brief as settled fact ("THIS COMMIT MAKES BOTH CLAIMS FALSE"). Escalating a question
while pre-answering it makes the escalation decorative - the car ships the guess before
the ruling lands. The owner then ruled the other way (shipped runtime versus test
toolchain; gate-triggered installs are legitimate; spirit met if not letter).
Aggravating: docs/setup.md already drew that exact line one sentence away. A near-miss,
cheap only because the owner was present and fast.

Statute index updated.
'@
git log --oneline -3

record forever. Same here - the valve exists so the substrate can deform honestly. Which
is why the ruling gets written down with its reasoning every time, and why "spirit met,
not letter" is an argument for PRECISION in the amendment and never a licence for silence
about it. A bend nobody can find later is not a bend; it is a crack.

content.text:
That's the piece my section was missing, and it's the better half of the rule.

I'd written *escalate, don't infer* without saying why the shop needs a bend path at all — which left it reading as pure restriction. Your framing inverts it: the valve isn't a weakening of the rules, it's what keeps them honest. **A process with no legitimate way to bend doesn't stay unbent.** It gets bent anyway, silently, by whoever hits the wall first with work to deliver. The pressure exists whether or not there's somewhere for it to go. Give it a named outlet and the bend becomes a recorded ruling with reasoning attached; deny it one and the same bend happens off the record, unauditable. Rigidity doesn't prevent exceptions — it only prevents you from seeing them.

I tied it to the founding vocabulary rather than leaving it as a floating metaphor, because it's already in there: this is **geological development's own law of change** — metamorphism under measured pressure, never rewriting a stratum in place — applied to a single decision instead of a document. Rock bends under load rather than shattering, and the bend stays legible forever. That's also the guard against the valve being abused: the ruling gets written down with its reasoning every time, which is why "spirit met, not letter" argues for *precision* in the amendment and never for silence. **A bend nobody can find later is not a bend; it is a crack.**

Landed in `CLAUDE.md` with the statute-index row extended, and in my memory so it survives the session.

**Meanwhile, Car 26's unit closed clean.** APPROVE, zero Majors, merged at `8c9c434`, #26 closed. The review's standout: it independently corroborated the car's disclosed 56-vs-52 discrepancy by spotting that `body_file` holds constant at 25 across all three measurements while `model` climbs — because the migration ran once historically and can't grow, while `model` is written per dispatch. That constancy is what made the explanation *true* rather than merely plausible, and it's the kind of check that separates verification from agreement. It also watched the detector still fire on junk fields, which was the whole point of the ticket — the conditions had to stop being *false*, not stop being *shown*.

Two more findings filed rather than folded: **#38** (`index-format.md` owns canonical ordering but is silent on the new fields) and **#39** — a pre-existing Law 1 falsehood in a code comment claiming "the board's model-mix rendering consumes it" when no consumer exists anywhere. That one was exposed by the reviewer's own sentence trace, three lines from a line the car cited.

Car 33 is still running and holds the last train slot. `dev` is pushed with CI being watched to a recorded terminal state.
ok playwright will make our adversarial reviews much tighter and more agressive now
Right for one class, and I want to sharpen where — plus name a risk that runs the other way.

input.command:
cd C:\Users\Chris\git\starcar
$b = @'
OWNER OBSERVATION, 2026-07-23: "playwright will make our adversarial reviews much tighter and more aggressive now."

Filed under never-drop, because the observation implies a CARRIER CHANGE (the reviewer brief template) and a carrier change that lives only in conversation does not bind anyone. TRIGGER: #33 merges. Not before - writing "reviewers must assert computed style" while no browser driver exists would describe behavior the software does not have, which Law 1 forbids ("true always; complete only when the thing exists").

WHAT ACTUALLY CHANGES, precisely - the TIER, not the effort

Today, "the board renders calm work calmly" is guarded by a REVIEWER'S DILIGENCE. The Car 31 reviewer had to hand-build a browser: it enumerated all 11 `color:` declarations in board.css, verified mechanically that its model captured every one, walked ancestors for 216 text-bearing elements, and computed effective colour itself. That is excellent work and it is a SIMULATION of a cascade, not a measurement of one.

After #33 the same claim is settled by `getComputedStyle(el).color` - one call, from the real engine that ships the rule. That moves the class from ATTENTION-TIER to MECHANISM-TIER, and the Healing Loop explicitly ranks a test above a review rule. `CLAUDE.md`'s NORTH STAR section already books this exact debt in the doc-claims case: "That is attention-tier where CI would be mechanical, which is a real downgrade and is recorded as one." #33 repays part of that debt for the visual surface.

SCOPE HONESTLY BOUNDED - it sharpens ONE class, not review generally

Today's two reviews (Car 31, Car 26) produced 4 Minors and 6 Notes between them. By my count exactly ONE - Car 31's MINOR-1, the `color: inherit` hole a text regex cannot resolve - would have been FOUND by a browser. The rest came from opening files and re-deriving numbers: a false code comment (#39), a silent ordering owner (#38), unpinned taxonomy mirrors (#37), verified arithmetic (247+2=249), and hop-by-hop provenance tracing to a terminus. Playwright does nothing for any of those. "Much tighter" is true for the visual surface and roughly neutral elsewhere, and saying so keeps the instrument's reputation calibrated - which this shop cares about more than most.

THE RISK THAT RUNS THE OTHER WAY, and it is the reason this ticket is not just a template edit

A measurement instrument can make a review LAZIER rather than tighter. If `getComputedStyle` returns the nominal colour, a reviewer may stop there. Today's reviewer did not stop: it enumerated WHICH 61 elements remained red and RULED on each - 54 board-conditions correct because the server genuinely raised them, 3 lane-titles design-consistent per the mockup brief's most-severe rule, and `.track-title` an open edge (now #36). A tool that answers "what colour IS it" does not answer "what colour SHOULD it be." The second question is the adversarial one, and it is the one that cannot be automated.

So the template change must ADD a mechanical floor without REMOVING the judgment requirement.

PROPOSED CARRIER CHANGE (docs/templates/car-brief.md, reviewer addendum), to land WITH or AFTER #33:
1. A new line in the same shape as the existing "RUN YOURSELF at HEAD, expect counts, report observed": any claim about what the board RENDERS is settled by computed-style measurement against the real served board, never by reading CSS or by reasoning about the cascade. Report observed values.
2. Explicitly PRESERVED, not replaced: the reviewer still rules on whether the observed rendering is CORRECT against the design authority (the mockup brief, the three-register law, the issue's own text). Measurement establishes what is; judgment establishes what ought to be. A verdict that reports colours without ruling on them is a spelling check with better instrumentation.
3. Consider whether the GUARD CHECK line should require that any new visual guard be fault-injected THROUGH THE BROWSER, since #31 proved a text-level guard can pass while the defect is live.

Related: #35 (the regex guard's `color: inherit` hole, which a computed-style assertion subsumes entirely), #36 (the residue ruling that needed judgment, not measurement).
'@
gh issue create --title "After #33: require computed-style evidence in reviewer briefs - add the mechanical floor WITHOUT removing the judgment requirement" --body $b --label "area:process" | Tee-Object -Variable u
gh project item-add 6 --owner polecatspeaks --url $u | Out-Null

Agreed for one class — and I filed it as **#40** rather than leaving it in chat, because your observation implies a *carrier change* (the reviewer brief template), and a carrier change that lives only in conversation binds nobody. Trigger is "#33 merges," not now: writing "reviewers must assert computed style" while no browser driver exists would describe behavior the software doesn't have.

**The sharper version of what changes: the tier, not the effort.** Today "the board renders calm work calmly" is guarded by a reviewer's diligence. Car 31's reviewer literally hand-built a browser — enumerated all 11 `color:` declarations in `board.css`, verified mechanically that its model caught every one, then walked ancestors for 216 elements computing effective colour itself. Excellent work, and a *simulation* of a cascade rather than a measurement of one. After #33 it's one `getComputedStyle` call from the engine that ships the rule. That moves the class from attention-tier to mechanism-tier, and the Healing Loop ranks a test above a review rule. `CLAUDE.md` already books this exact debt in the doc-claims case — "attention-tier where CI would be mechanical, a real downgrade, recorded as one" — so #33 repays part of it.

**Where I'd bound the claim:** it sharpens the visual surface and is roughly neutral elsewhere. Today's two reviews produced 4 Minors and 6 Notes; by my count exactly *one* — Car 31's `color: inherit` hole — would have been found by a browser. The rest came from opening files and re-deriving numbers: the false code comment in #39, the silent ordering owner in #38, unpinned taxonomy mirrors in #37, the 247+2=249 arithmetic, the hop-by-hop trace to a terminus. Playwright touches none of those.

**And the risk that runs the opposite direction, which is why #40 isn't just a template edit:** a measurement instrument can make reviews *lazier*. If `getComputedStyle` returns the nominal colour, a reviewer may stop there. Today's reviewer didn't — it enumerated which 61 elements stayed red and ruled on each: 54 board-conditions correct because the server genuinely raised them, 3 lane titles design-consistent under the mockup brief's most-severe rule, and `.track-title` an open edge that became #36. **A tool that answers "what colour is it" does not answer "what colour should it be."** The second question is the adversarial one and it can't be automated. So #40 specifies adding the mechanical floor *without* removing the judgment requirement — a verdict that reports colours without ruling on them is a spelling check with better instrumentation.

CI is green on the doctrine push (`6f5068d`, recorded terminal state). Car 33 is still running with the last train slot; when it reports, its Opus review closes the unit and #40's trigger fires.

input.command:
cd C:\Users\Chris\git\starcar
$c = @'
CONDUCTOR SELF-CORRECTION, prompted by the owner: "playwright gives the adversary better tooling to dig out those majors for us."

The owner is right and my scoping above was too conservative. I wrote that Playwright is "roughly neutral" outside the visual surface, and I derived that from counting today's actual findings - 1 of 4 Minors would have been browser-found. THAT COUNT IS BIASED BY CONSTRUCTION: it measures what the current instruments DID find, which by definition excludes what they CANNOT see. This log's own founding meta-class says it plainly - "a red never looked at is indistinguishable from no red, and an absence is invisible unless something asserts completeness." I committed that error one level up, against the instrument set itself.

A better instrument does not only answer existing questions faster. It makes whole ATTACK CLASSES newly reachable. Enumerating the ones a real browser opens, none of which any current test can touch:

1. VISIBILITY, OCCLUSION AND CONTRAST - the HAVAGLANCE class, and the sharpest one.
   A board condition can be present in the DOM, correctly registered, verbatim, and STILL invisible to a human: clipped by an overflow, occluded by a sibling, rendered off-panel, or drawn in a colour whose contrast against its background is unreadable. Every existing test would pass. The board's entire thesis is TOTAL TRANSMISSION IN A SINGLE GLANCE, so a disclosure the eye cannot find is a Law 1 defect wearing a passing test suite. Playwright can assert bounding boxes, actual visibility, and computed contrast. Today NOTHING can. This is a Major class that is currently unreachable, not merely expensive.

2. THE FULL SENTENCE FLOW BECOMES ATTACKABLE, not just traceable.
   Today the sentence check is READ hop by hop, file:line. With a browser the adversary can INJECT at the store and OBSERVE at the pixel - a true end-to-end fault injection across every process boundary at once. That is precisely the shape of this shop's most expensive scar: nine wire fields crossed sixteen correct, individually-reviewed files, and a third process hop silently dropped all nine because every test read one page of a three-page sentence. The first end-to-end reader was production. A browser makes THIS surface end-to-end attackable at review time.

3. DYNAMIC AND FAILURE STATES, observed as the user sees them.
   The board has SSE, a disconnect watchdog, reconnect, stale marking, and last-known-good display. Those are currently guarded by unit tests of the PIECES (sse-protocol.test.js and the minidom shim). A reviewer with a browser can kill the stream mid-flight and observe whether the honesty chrome ACTUALLY appears, whether stale data is ACTUALLY marked, and whether the board degrades the way the gating matrix says it must. The honesty chrome is a Law 1 surface and it has never been observed end-to-end under real failure.

4. THE DISCOVERY PATH, end to end.
   "An unrecognised id renders hot, BY NAME, verbatim, never silently as calm" is the discovery rule. It is unit-tested at vocab.js. Whether an unknown value actually SURFACES to a human through the full stack - store, fold, assemble, wire, view, CSS - has never been observed. Inject an unknown outcome into the store, look at the screen.

5. COMPLETENESS RENDERED VERSUS DECLARED.
   checkLaneCompleteness compares config.laneCount against observed lanes and raises a condition. Whether that condition is VISIBLE when it fires is item 1's problem again, and it is the guard against a silently missing lane - the thing the lane count exists for.

So the corrected position: Playwright's value is not mainly that it settles the cascade question faster. It is that it hands the adversary a class of attack it currently cannot perform AT ALL - observing the product as a human receives it, under injected failure. The reviewer-brief change proposed above should therefore not stop at "assert computed style"; it should name these attack surfaces explicitly, or reviewers will use the new tool only for the question that motivated it.

WHAT DOES NOT CHANGE, and it is the reason the judgment requirement stays: every one of the five above still ends in a RULING - is this visible ENOUGH, is this degradation CORRECT, should this have surfaced. The tool makes the observation possible; it never makes the judgment. Both halves land in the brief, or the instrument makes reviews faster instead of tighter.
'@
gh issue comment 40 --body $c | Out-Null; Write-Host "self-correction recorded on #40"
input.description: Record the corrected, expanded value case on #40

input.command:
cd C:\Users\Chris\git\starcar
git add docs/friction-log.md
git commit -q -m @'
retro: I estimated a new instrument's yield from the yield of the instruments that could not see

Owner correction, one sentence: "playwright gives the adversary better tooling to dig out
those majors for us."

I had scoped #33 as "one class, roughly neutral elsewhere" and derived that by counting
today'"'"'s actual findings - 1 of 4 Minors would have been browser-found. That count is
biased BY CONSTRUCTION. It measures what the current instruments DID find, which by
definition excludes what they CANNOT see. This is the friction log'"'"'s own founding
meta-class - "a red never looked at is indistinguishable from no red; an absence is
invisible unless something asserts completeness" - applied one level up, to the
instrument set itself rather than to a finding.

Re-derived properly, a real browser opens at least five attack classes that are
currently UNREACHABLE rather than merely expensive:
1. Visibility, occlusion and contrast. A board condition can be present in the DOM,
   correctly registered and verbatim, yet clipped, occluded, off-panel, or drawn at
   unreadable contrast. Every existing test passes. HAVAGLANCE fails outright. A
   disclosure the eye cannot find is a Law 1 defect wearing a green suite.
2. End-to-end store-to-pixel fault injection - the nine-wire-fields scar class, where
   sixteen individually-correct reviewed files hid a third-process drop because every
   test read one page of a three-page sentence.
3. Dynamic failure states observed as the USER sees them - kill the SSE stream, watch
   whether the honesty chrome actually appears and stale data is actually marked. Those
   are currently guarded only by unit tests of the pieces.
4. The discovery path end to end - "an unrecognised id renders hot, BY NAME" is
   unit-tested at vocab.js and has never been observed surfacing through the full stack.
5. Rendered-versus-declared lane completeness, which is item 1 again and is the guard
   against a silently missing lane.

Recorded on #40 with the scope correction, so the reviewer-brief change names these
attack surfaces explicitly - otherwise reviewers will use the new tool only for the
question that motivated it. Unchanged: every one still ends in a RULING. The tool makes
the observation possible; it never makes the judgment.
'@
git push origin dev 2>&1 | Select-Object -Last 2; git rev-parse --short HEAD
